materials.label: Mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา

samples: Lik phan khao, Lokatham paet poeng

lede: Katha (Pali spell-formulae) accounts for 184 catalogued manuscripts, 76 of them flagged research-priority. It clusters in Nan (27% of the corpus for this subject), ahead of Phrae and Lampang. Nearly all (89%) are written in Tham Lanna · อักษรธรรมล้านนา script. By support it leans to palm-leaf · ใบลาน (55%) over mulberry paper (saa) · กระดาษสา (41%). Dated witnesses run 1549–1974 CE (68 of 184 carry a date).

findings.title: Where the drawn tradition is thickest
findings.gloss: Manuscripts carrying the most diagram pages — the yantra, cosmological charts, and talismanic figures that are drawn, not written.

findings.title: Already legible to a machine
findings.gloss: The manuscripts with the most recognised Thai text — the leading edge of the whole point: making a living tradition readable to a model and not only to a scholar.

authored.title: Katha (Pali spell-formulae)
authored.body_html:
What this is

A katha (gāthā) is a spoken formula — a short Pali verse said aloud to make something happen. It is the atomic unit of the northern saiyasat: the words a monk or mo chants to protect, to bless, to bind, to heal, to summon. Unlike a genre, a katha is not a kind of book — it is a kind of utterance, and that is exactly why it turns up everywhere. The catalogue holds 179 witnesses whose titles name a katha, and they scatter across the whole collection rather than sitting in one shelf.

The connective tissue of the corpus

The single most important fact about katha is its genre spread. The 179 witnesses fall under buddhist_canonical (55), astrology (44), magic & ritual (28), jātaka (26) and other (18) — no genre owns it, because every genre uses it. The same formula-technology is the recited verse of scripture on one leaf, the efficacious spell of the wichaa on the next. This is the clearest single demonstration of the project's governing thesis: that canonical Buddhism and efficacious saiyasat are one performance seen from two sides. Read this entity as the thread that stitches the chanting liturgy, the astrology and the magic together.

Two registers sit inside the count. On the learned side, the nissaya katha — Nitsai katha thammapata, Panca nibat katha, Thatukatha — Pali verse glossed and parsed for study. On the efficacious side, the sainyasat katha — Katha ongkan (invocation of the Buddha), Katha o, Katha sao hum, the untitled bundles labelled Katha tang tang ("various katha"). The learned katha is read; the efficacious katha is deployed.

The shape of the collection

Its physical profile breaks the scholastic mould in a telling way. Support is palm-leaf (102) against a very heavy mulberry-paper (75) — far more paper than the canonical or grammar corpora, exactly as one expects of formulae that were opened and used rather than shelved. Script is 92% Tham Lanna but with a notable 19 monolingual Pali witnesses — the katha is Pali, so a share needs no vernacular at all. It reaches back to 1549 and forward to 1974, and 72 of the 179 are flagged research-priority. Holdings lead at Wat Sung Men (38) but spread widely — Wat Si Mongkhon, Wat Lai Hin Luang, Wat Phra That Lampang Luang.

How katha works — from the digested textbooks

Corpus — this section quotes the contributed printed wichaa textbooks (OCR-digested into the `pages` table), a body distinct from the palm-leaf catalogue the counts above measure. Where the catalogue tells us katha travel bundled and cut across genre, the textbooks show us the thing the title line cannot: what a katha actually does on the page. Citations give slug and page; densest is compendium-of-ancient-katha ("ประมวลคาถาตำราโบราณ", "compendium of ancient katha"), effectively a formulary.

  • The recipe grammar. A katha entry is a two-part unit: a Pali (or pseudo-Pali) formula, then a plain-Thai rubric of the form "คาถานี้ … ดีแล" — "this katha [does X], and it is good." The verse is inert until the rubric tells you the act it powers. The book is not a scripture to read but a procedure list to perform.
  • The katha activates a substance. The operative verb is เสก (sek, "to enchant by uttering over"): the reciter enchants lime, betel, wax, water — "เสกปูน … ใส่โอ่งน้ำ" ("enchant lime … put it in the water-jar"), "เสกหมาก" ("enchant betel"), "เสกขี้ผึ้ง … เสกน้ำล้างหน้า" ("enchant wax … enchant face-washing water") (compendium-of-ancient-katha p77, p38). The word does the work through a material — the same coupling of formula-and-object that binds katha to yantra and takrut in the collection.
  • The effect palette, made operational. The rubrics sort by outcome, and they are exactly the merit-words that recur corpus-wide. Metta / attraction: "เขารักเราใจจะขาด" — "they will love us to the breaking of the heart" (p77). Invulnerability (คงทน / คงกระพัน): a titled "คาถาคงทน" ("katha of endurance") whose rubric is "เสกสารพัดคงทนดีแล" — "enchants all-round endurance" (p38). Weapon-warding: "ภาวนากันอาวุธทั้งปวงมิต้องเลย" — "recite to ward every weapon; none can strike" (p38).
  • Totalising protection. The invulnerability katha covers every posture and state at once: "คงทั้งหน้าคงทั้งหลัง คงทั้งนั่งคงทั้งยืน คงทั้งหลับคงทั้งตื่น" — "durable front and back, sitting and standing, sleeping and waking" (compendium p31). The formula does not protect an action; it armours a whole life.

Inference — read together, the textbook rubrics confirm the catalogue thesis from the inside: katha is the operative verb of the wichaa, and its grammar (formula → substance → named effect) is stable enough across these printed books to be treated as a genuine ritual technology, not a loose devotional habit.

Notes

  • The household-manual signature. Look at the bundled titles: Nyan lae katha (yantra and katha), Katha tang tang, Sainyasat, Tholanisan, Katha tang tang, Su khwan cao fa. Katha travels bound to yantra, holasat and su khwan soul-calling — the recurring ritual-manual object where formula, diagram and calendar share one book. When a katha title also names a nyan or a sainyasat element, the object is almost certainly a working wichaa manual, not a scholastic reader.
  • The alias problem is acute here. Katha / gāthā / gatha and the compounds (Kathapan, katha ongkan) fragment across transliterations; the count here is a heuristic title-match and is indicative, not exact. A dedicated pass to normalise the spellings, and to separate the nissaya katha from the sainyasat katha, is the obvious next step and would split a learned tradition from an efficacious one.
  • A crawl priority in its own right. Because katha is the operative verb of the wichaa — the actual words that do the work — it deserves targeted collection: any manuscript that records what was said is a primary source for the living tradition this archive documents.
